candidate_problems.text: How much explanatory work is really being done by weak iteration/observability/future-coupling versus classic preference heterogeneity, wealth constraints, coercive enforcement costs, and production-technology features of public goods? The conjecture risks being too broad unless it specifies when structural reciprocity failure is the dominant cause rather than one contributing factor.

What are the precise boundary conditions under which institutions that increase visibility, memory, and future consequences genuinely stabilize public-good provision, rather than merely relabeling the good as excludable or shifting provision onto coercion? This matters because clubs, subscriptions, reputation systems, and taxation may succeed through different mechanisms, and the conjecture needs a sharper account of which mechanism is load-bearing.

How should the conjecture handle large-scale, one-shot, or highly diffuse public goods where contributors and beneficiaries are separated across time, space, and identity (for example climate mitigation or basic research)? If reciprocity requires repeated, monitorable interaction, the open question is whether there exist scalable substitutes for reciprocity or whether the conjecture implies hard limits on non-coercive provision in such domains.
